paper.title: FlashOptim: Optimizers for Memory Efficient Training
paper.abstract: Standard mixed-precision training of neural networks requires many bytes of accelerator memory for each model parameter. These bytes reflect not just the parameter itself, but also its gradient and one or more optimizer state variables. With each of these values typically requiring 4 bytes, training even a 7 billion parameter model can be impractical for researchers with less than 100GB of accelerator memory.   We introduce FlashOptim, a suite of optimizations that reduces per-parameter memory b

memory_insight.compression_quantization_methods: 16-bit gradients, 8-bit optimizer state quantization, master weight splitting, companding functions
memory_insight.before_after_comparisons: 16 bytes -> 7 bytes per parameter (AdamW), 5 bytes with gradient release

analysis.engineering_takeaway: Implementing master weight splitting and companding functions can reduce memory usage by over 50%.
technical_details: Exploiting a tight bound on quantization error
Designing companding functions for 8-bit optimizer state quantization
analysis.deployment_feasibility: Yes, but may require careful optimization and tuning for specific hardware configurations.

review_questions: Is this genuinely a breakthrough? Or incremental improvement?
Is the DRAM impact claim supported by benchmarks?
